content: When you are building Rust libraries that must interoperate with C, C++, or other systems languages, the bridge between high-level abstractions and raw memory is often an enum. The core decision every developer faces is whether to use #[repr(C)] or #[repr(u8)], a choice dictated entirely by how the downstream system expects data to be laid out in memory. The #[repr(C)] attribute forces the enum variant discriminants to occupy contiguous, zero-padded slots starting at address 0, aligning exactly with the C ABI rules that require explicit alignment and padding for safety. In contrast, #[repr(u8)] tells the compiler to treat every variant as a single byte of data, packing them tightly together without any padding bytes between discriminants; this is an optimization strategy that sacrifices alignment guarantees in exchange for smaller memory footprints and faster access patterns when dealing with large enumeration sets. The distinction becomes critical when you consider transmuting raw pointers or casting integers to enums; if you choose #[repr(C)] but your C code expects a single byte per variant, your pointer arithmetic will misalign the data, leading to undefined behavior when that memory is read by foreign functions. Conversely, using #[repr(u8)] on an enum with variants larger than one byte might cause silent truncation or unexpected bit patterns if the downstream system relies on strict 1-byte alignment for its own internal logic. The choice between these two approaches depends heavily on whether you need to preserve native word boundaries or maximize storage efficiency. For instance, consider a scenario where your Rust struct contains an enum field used as an index into a lookup table; here #[repr(u8)] is preferable because it ensures the integer value matches exactly with the byte stored in memory, eliminating any padding overhead that #[repr(C)] would introduce on 64-bit systems. However, if you are interfacing directly with a C library where the caller expects to read the enum as an integer but also requires strict adherence to the host machine's calling convention regarding alignment, then #[repr(C)] is mandatory because it guarantees that the discriminant occupies the correct number of bytes and respects the required padding rules for the specific target architecture. A common pitfall developers encounter is assuming that #[repr(u8)] automatically means one byte per variant regardless of the underlying word size; in reality, while the compiler ensures the enum value fits into a single byte, the memory layout still adheres to the alignment constraints of the host platform unless you explicitly disable padding with additional attributes like #[repr(C, packed)]. Another layer of complexity arises when dealing with negative integers or non-integer types inside enums; #[repr(u8)] enforces unsigned behavior strictly, preventing any accidental overflow or sign extension issues that might occur with signed discriminants in other repr configurations. If your C code performs arithmetic on these enum values before passing them back to Rust, the lack of padding in a #[repr(u8)] declaration means those calculations happen purely at the byte level without interference from implicit alignment checks found in #[repr(C)]. Finally, when you need to implement traits like From or Into for conversion between integer types and your custom enums, the repr attribute determines whether the trait implementation can safely cast arbitrary integers into valid variants or if it must perform bounds checking against the specific discriminant range allowed by that representation. In summary, select #[repr(u8)] when memory efficiency and simple byte-level indexing are paramount and you control all downstream access patterns, but default to #[repr(C)] whenever interoperating with generic C code where strict alignment and predictable padding are non-negotiable requirements for safety.
